questions.question: When you inject `ILogger<OrderService>`, what determines the logger's **category** (the key that per-category level filtering uses)?
options: The fully-qualified type name of the generic argument `T` (e.g. `MyApp.Services.OrderService`)
The `EventId` passed to each log call
The name of the registered logging provider (Console, Debug, etc.)
The assembly name of the project being logged from
questions.explanation: The generic argument of `ILogger<T>` sets the category to the fully-qualified type name, which is what per-category minimum-level configuration keys off. EventId, provider name, and assembly name do not set the category.

questions.question: Why is `_logger.LogInformation("User {UserId} placed order {OrderId}", userId, orderId)` preferred over `_logger.LogInformation($"User {userId} placed order {orderId}")`?
options: The template form captures `UserId` and `OrderId` as queryable structured properties and gives the event a stable message id
The template form is the only syntax that compiles in .NET 6 and later
Interpolation logs at a lower severity level than templates
Interpolation cannot include more than one placeholder per message
questions.explanation: The message-template form preserves each value as a structured, queryable field and shares one stable template id across all such events. The interpolated form bakes a flat string the backend can only substring-search. The other options are false (both compile, neither changes level, and interpolation supports many placeholders).

questions.question: A retry kicked in and the operation then succeeded via a fallback path. Which level is the appropriate choice for that log?
options: Warning
Error
Critical
Information
questions.explanation: Warning is for unexpected-but-recovered situations such as a retry or fallback. Error means the current operation failed, Critical is an app/host-level failure or data loss, and Information is normal noteworthy flow.

questions.question: What does `_logger.BeginScope(...)` accomplish?
options: It attaches a set of properties to every log written inside the `using` block
It raises the minimum log level for the duration of the block
It buffers log records and flushes them only when the scope disposes
It creates a new logging provider scoped to the current request
questions.explanation: A scope stamps its properties onto every log line emitted inside the block, so correlation fields appear without repeating them. It does not change levels, buffer/flush records, or create providers.

questions.question: In .NET's OpenTelemetry mapping, an `Activity` started from an `ActivitySource` is an OpenTelemetry **span**.
questions.explanation: OpenTelemetry in .NET sits on the BCL diagnostics primitives: `ActivitySource`/`Activity` is the tracing API and an `Activity` *is* a span.

questions.question: Given the call below, which structured property captures the value of `userId`?
options: `OrderId`, because placeholders bind positionally and `userId` is the first argument
`UserId`, because the property name matches the variable name `userId`
Neither â€” the runtime throws because the names are mismatched
Both `OrderId` and `UserId`, because the value is duplicated into each field
questions.explanation: Placeholders bind positionally, not by name. The first `{OrderId}` binds to the first argument `userId`, so `userId`'s value is stored under the property `OrderId` â€” a classic mismatch bug. The names are only labels for the structured output; no exception is thrown.

questions.question: With this configuration, a log written by category `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Routing` at `Information` level is:
options: Suppressed, because the most specific matching prefix `Microsoft.AspNetCore` sets the minimum to Warning
Written, because `Default` is Information and that always wins
Suppressed, because Information is below the global default minimum
Written, because category prefixes only affect Trace and Debug levels
questions.explanation: Filtering is hierarchical by category prefix and the most specific match wins. `Microsoft.AspNetCore` (a prefix of the category) sets the minimum to Warning, so an Information record is suppressed. `Default` is only the fallback when no prefix matches.

questions.question: Which statements about the `[LoggerMessage]` source generator are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: The enclosing class and the logging method must both be declared `partial`
It performs the level check before doing any argument work, so a filtered-out call is essentially free
It avoids boxing value-type arguments into an `object[]`
It requires the OpenTelemetry SDK to be registered before it will emit logs
It parses the message template once at compile time rather than on every call
questions.explanation: The generator emits a `partial` method body (class and method must be `partial`), checks the level first, avoids boxing, and pre-parses the template at compile time. It is independent of OpenTelemetry â€” it works with any provider and needs no OTel SDK.

questions.question: In a `[LoggerMessage]` method whose `Message` has no `{...}` placeholder for it, how is an `Exception` parameter handled?
options: It is bound automatically as the log entry's exception, not as a template argument
It is rendered into the message via its `ToString()` as an extra positional argument
It causes a compile error because every parameter must have a matching placeholder
It is silently ignored and never recorded
questions.explanation: An `Exception` parameter is matched automatically and logged as the entry's exception, not consumed as a template placeholder. It is neither rendered as a positional arg, an error, nor dropped.

questions.question: Why should you avoid putting unbounded values like user ids or full URLs into **metric** labels/tags?
options: They explode cardinality, which drives up the cost and degrades the metrics backend
Metric tags are write-only and can never be queried back
Tags are limited to numeric values, so strings are dropped
It would force every metric to be exported as a trace span
questions.explanation: Metrics are cheap only when label cardinality stays bounded. High-cardinality values like user ids or URLs multiply time series, exploding cost and load; such detail belongs in logs/traces. The other options are not real constraints of the metrics model.

questions.question: You created a `static readonly ActivitySource("MyApp.Orders")` and start spans from it, but none appear in your backend. The OTLP exporter is wired and ASP.NET Core spans show up fine. What is the most likely cause?
options: You never called `.AddSource("MyApp.Orders")` on the tracing builder, so the source's spans are dropped
An `ActivitySource` cannot be `static` and must instead be created per request to emit spans
Custom `ActivitySource` spans need a separate exporter from the auto-instrumentation's exporter
The source name must exactly equal the project's assembly name or its spans are discarded
questions.explanation: The SDK only listens to sources you register; you must `AddSource(name)` matching the `ActivitySource` name or its spans are silently dropped. Static singletons are correct and recommended, one exporter handles all spans, and the name need not match the assembly.

questions.question: Service A calls Service B over HTTP, both using the standard OpenTelemetry instrumentation. How does B's span end up in the **same** distributed trace as A's?
options: `HttpClient` instrumentation injects the W3C `traceparent` header outbound and ASP.NET Core extracts it inbound, continuing the same `TraceId`
Both services independently generate the same `TraceId` from a shared random seed
The OTel Collector merges traces by matching request timestamps
A custom GUID is passed in the request body and parsed by B
questions.explanation: W3C trace context travels in the `traceparent` header: HttpClient instrumentation injects it on the way out and ASP.NET Core instrumentation extracts it on the way in, so B continues A's `TraceId` as a child span. The other mechanisms are not how distributed tracing correlates spans.

questions.question: A single `/health` endpoint runs all checks, including a DB ping. A transient DB blip causes a pod restart loop. What is the correct fix?
options: Split into a cheap dependency-free **liveness** endpoint and a separate **readiness** endpoint that includes the DB check, selected by tags
Increase the liveness probe timeout so the DB ping always completes
Move the DB ping into the liveness check so failures are caught earlier
Return `Degraded` from every check so the orchestrator never restarts the pod
questions.explanation: Conflating liveness and readiness means a dependency failure trips the liveness probe and the orchestrator restarts a healthy process. Splitting them with tags makes a DB blip only stop traffic (readiness) without restarting (liveness). Raising timeouts or moving the DB check into liveness makes it worse.

questions.question: This loop dominates a CPU profile in production even though `Debug` is disabled there. What is the senior explanation and fix?
questions.code:
for (var i = 0; i < items.Length; i++)
    _logger.LogDebug("Item {Index} of {Total}: {@Item}", i, items.Length, items[i]);
options: Args are boxed into `object[]` and `{@Item}` set up before the filter short-circuits; guard with `IsEnabled` or use `[LoggerMessage]`
`LogDebug` ignores configuration and always writes, so the call must be removed entirely
Destructuring with `{@Item}` is what enables the level filter, so dropping `@` makes it free
The cost is the disk I/O of each written line; redirecting the Console output fixes it
questions.explanation: Each convenience call boxes value-type args into an `object[]` and prepares destructuring before the level filter fully short-circuits the cost; over a tight loop that is expensive. Guarding with `IsEnabled` and/or using `[LoggerMessage]` (which checks the level before any argument work) eliminates it. `LogDebug` does honor configuration, `@` does not control filtering, and no I/O happens when Debug is off.

questions.question: Using `$"..."` string interpolation to build a log message pays the formatting cost **even when** that log level is filtered out and nothing is written.
questions.explanation: Interpolation produces the finished string before the logging call is even reached, so the formatting cost is paid unconditionally. Message templates defer rendering until a provider that passed the filter actually needs the string.

questions.question: Which statements about `Activity`/sampling in .NET OpenTelemetry are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: `ActivitySource.StartActivity` can return `null` when no listener is sampling, which is why `activity?.` is idiomatic
Sampling is decided at the root span and propagated, so child spans honor the parent's decision
`ActivitySource` and `Meter` are cheap to construct, so per-request instances are recommended
When `StartActivity` returns `null`, the unsampled span costs essentially nothing
Each child span independently re-rolls its own sampling decision regardless of the parent
questions.explanation: `StartActivity` returns `null` when nothing is sampling (hence `activity?.`), the unsampled span is essentially free, and the sampling decision is made at the root and propagated so children honor the parent. `ActivitySource`/`Meter` are expensive and should be static singletons, and children do not re-roll independently.

questions.question: You switched the host to Serilog. Application code still calls `_logger.BeginScope(...)`, but the scope properties no longer appear as fields in Seq. Which Serilog configuration is required to surface them?
options: `Enrich.FromLogContext()`
`WriteTo.Seq(...)` with `IncludeScopes = true`
`ReadFrom.Services(services)`
`MinimumLevel.Override("Default", LogEventLevel.Debug)`
questions.explanation: `Enrich.FromLogContext()` is what makes `ILogger.BeginScope(...)` values flow into Serilog events as structured fields. `IncludeScopes` is a Console (Microsoft.Extensions.Logging) option, `ReadFrom.Services` pulls DI services, and minimum-level overrides only affect filtering.

questions.question: You wrap several log calls in `BeginScope(new { OrderId = id })`, but the Console output shows the log messages with no `OrderId` field, while your Seq sink shows it fine. What is the most likely reason?
options: The Console provider does not render scopes unless `IncludeScopes = true` is set; structured sinks capture them by default
Scopes are only honored by the first registered provider, so Console is skipped
Anonymous-object scopes are dropped; only `Dictionary<string,object>` scopes work anywhere
Console flushes asynchronously and discards scope properties under load
questions.explanation: The Console provider must opt in with `IncludeScopes = true` to render scope properties; structured backends like Seq/Serilog/OTel capture scope values as fields regardless. Scopes are not limited to one provider or to dictionary form, and Console does not drop scope data under load.
